attributes.stem:
A six-year-old boy is admitted to hospital for tonsillectomy. He has a history of recurrent tonsillitis but no other medical conditions. He does not take any regular medications. His parent reports he had a mild cold two weeks ago but is now well. His immunisations are up to date.

On examination, he appears well with vital signs within normal limits. During emergence from general anaesthesia, immediately after extubation, he develops sudden onset stridor with marked suprasternal and intercostal recession. His oxygen saturation rapidly drops to 85%.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate first step in management?
options: Continuous positive airway pressure with 100% oxygen
attributes.explanation:
This clinical scenario describes laryngospasm, a reflexive closure of the vocal cords that is more common in paediatric patients. The timing (during emergence), recent upper respiratory tract infection, and airway manipulation are all risk factors. The presentation with stridor and rapid desaturation is characteristic.

Continuous positive airway pressure with 100% oxygen using a tight-fitting face mask is the first-line management for laryngospasm. This technique helps to break the spasm by applying pressure against the closed vocal cords while ensuring maximal oxygenation.

Immediate reintubation would be technically challenging during active laryngospasm and could worsen the situation by causing trauma to the vocal cords. It should be considered only if initial management fails and the patient cannot be oxygenated.

Intramuscular suxamethonium is a rescue measure for severe laryngospasm that doesn't respond to continuous positive airway pressure. While effective, it requires subsequent airway management due to muscle paralysis and is not the first-line treatment.

Nebulised adrenaline would be appropriate for post-extubation stridor due to airway oedema but would not help with acute laryngospasm. It would also take too long to be effective in this acute situation.

Intravenous dexamethasone is useful for preventing post-extubation stridor but would not help with acute laryngospasm. Its onset of action is too slow for this emergency situation.

Prevention of laryngospasm includes ensuring an adequate depth of anaesthesia during airway manipulation and careful patient selection regarding recent upper respiratory tract infections. The anaesthetist should always be prepared for this common paediatric complication.
learningPoints: Laryngospasm is more common in paediatric patients due to their more reactive airways and relatively narrow upper airway anatomy.
Risk factors for laryngospasm include upper respiratory tract infections, airway manipulation, and light planes of anaesthesia.
Continuous positive airway pressure with 100% oxygen is the initial management for laryngospasm.
references: Alalami AA, Ayoub CM, Baraka AS. Laryngospasm: review of different prevention and treatment modalities. Paediatr Anaesth. 2008 Apr;18(4):281-8. doi: 10.1111/j.1460-9592.2008.02448.x. PMID: 18315632.

Collins S, Schedler P, Veasey B, Kristofy A, McDowell M. Prevention and Treatment of Laryngospasm in the Pediatric Patient: A Literature Review. AANA J. 2019 Apr;87(2):145-151. Erratum in: AANA J. 2019 Jun;87(3):229. PMID: 31587728.
